title: Strategic Non-Shareability of Quantum Correlations
abstract: Correlations distributed by a mediator are usually valued for the coordination they enable between authorized agents, but in adversarial settings a more decisive property is whether the same coordination can be inherited by an outside colluder without disturbing the authorized marginal. Classical shared randomness is freely copyable, so a hidden seed coordinating two agents can be duplicated for a third; entanglement is constrained by monogamy, which can forbid such lossless extensions in strongly nonlocal regimes. We turn this asymmetry into an operational resource for private-information games. For a fixed authorized behavior $P_{12}$, we define its \emph{collusive shadow} as the set of relabelled behaviors a colluder can reproduce in any admissible tripartite extension preserving $P_{12}$, and we identify \emph{strategic non-shareability} with the distance from this shadow. We prove that, on finite alphabets, the game-optimized anti-collusion capacity equals the total-variation distance to the shadow; a fixed game provides a task-specific separating witness, while optimization over relabelled games recovers the full distance. In the CHSH score slice, Toner--Verstraete monogamy yields the exact certified frontier, so the Bell local bound $S_{12}=2$ is the sharp onset of positive certified anti-collusion power, saturating at $1/(2\sqrt{2})$ for the maximally entangled CHSH strategy. Classical hidden-variable mediators have zero capacity in this slice. We complement these results with two operational tools: a Hoeffding-based finite-data certification protocol that turns observed Bell scores into confidence-bounded anti-collusion certificates, and a level-2 NPA semidefinite relaxation that extends certified upper envelopes to tilted Bell inequalities. These results recast entanglement monogamy as a measurable shareability deficit for quantum-mediated strategic networks.
summary: This paper develops a framework to quantify how quantum correlations can prevent third-party collusion. It demonstrates that quantum entanglement provides a unique 'anti-collusion power' that classical shared randomness lacks, specifically identifying the threshold where Bell non-locality enables certified security.
main_problem: Quantifying the 'strategic non-shareability' of quantum correlations, specifically measuring the degree to which a mediator's coordination can be protected from an outside colluder in adversarial settings.
main_result: The paper proves that anti-collusion capacity equals the total-variation distance to the collusive shadow and identifies the CHSH Bell local bound as the sharp onset of positive certified anti-collusion power.
method: The study uses a geometric approach based on total-variation distance, the NPA hierarchy (level-2 SDP relaxation) for bounding vulnerability, and Hoeffding-based protocols for finite-data certification.
model_or_system: A tripartite setup involving an authorized pair (Players 1 and 2) and a potential colluder (Player 3), utilizing both classical hidden-variable models and quantum models (including Werner states and tilted-CHSH inequalities).
key_observables: CHSH score (S12), collusive leakage score (C13), anti-collusion capacity (CAC), and collusive vulnerability.
important_parameters: CHSH score (s), visibility parameter (eta) for Werner states, and the tilt parameter (alpha) for tilted-CHSH inequalities.
main_assumptions: Assumes no-signalling consistency and finite alphabets; results for the CHSH slice rely on the Toner-Verstraete monogamy inequality.
figures_summary: Figure 1 visualizes the three-player game and classical vs. quantum collusion; Figure 2 shows the transition of anti-collusion power with noise; Figure 3 displays the Hoeffding-based certification and visibility thresholds; Figure 4 presents NPA upper bounds for tilted-CHSH inequalities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of strategic non-shareability, defines the mathematical framework of the collusive shadow, provides analytical proofs for the CHSH case, extends the analysis to tilted-CHSH via NPA hierarchy, and concludes with finite-data certification protocols.
why_interesting: It provides a quantitative resource theory for entanglement monogamy, framing it as a measurable 'shareability deficit' which is highly relevant for securing quantum networks and device-independent protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
